intro_text:
Teahupoʻo is a fishing village at the end of a one-lane road on the Taiarapu peninsula, the small southeastern lobe of Tahiti. The village has 1,455 residents, a Catholic chapel, a single grocery store, and a working pier. The wave that made the village's name a noun in surfing breaks roughly half a mile offshore, over a coral pass called Passe Havae. You cannot see it from the village without a boat. You can sometimes hear it.

The wave is not the largest in the world. Nazaré is taller; Cortes Bank is heavier in the abstract. What Teahupoʻo is, instead, is the most concentrated, most physically wrong-looking wave anyone has ever managed to ride. The seafloor goes from a thousand feet of open Pacific to about six feet of coral over the span of a few hundred yards, and there is no continental shelf to absorb the swell first. When a south-hemisphere groundswell arrives, the wave doesn't shoal — it folds. The crest stays above sea level while the trough scoops out below it, and the surfer rides what amounts to a square cylinder of water with a coral floor 51 centimeters under the lip's impact zone.

The Tahitian word *teahupoʻo* breaks into three roots: *te* (the), *ahu* (a heap, a mound, an altar), and *poʻo* (head). Most translations land somewhere between "the pile of heads" and "the wall of skulls." The folk etymology that surf media tells — that the name commemorates an ancient battle in which a king's heads-of-enemies were stacked at this spot, or that a son avenged his father by eating his killer's brain here — should be heard the way folk etymologies usually should: as oral tradition that has now become inseparable from the wave's mythology, regardless of whether it's literally true.

The modern surf history of Passe Havae starts in 1985, when a Tahitian named Thierry Vernaudon paddled out with a few friends. The first contest came in 1997. Brice Taerea, a Tahitian local, died there in 2000 — the only confirmed surfing fatality at the wave to date. Laird Hamilton's 17 August 2000 "Millennium Wave," shot by photographer Tim McKenna, was the moment the wave became globally legible. Kelly Slater has won the Tahiti Pro five times. Vahine Fierro became the first Tahitian to win the women's event in 2024. Kauli Vaast, born in Vairao, won the Olympic gold medal at Passe Havae on 5 August 2024.

The Olympic Games is also the reason the wave is now framed differently than it used to be. The fight over a permanent aluminum judges' tower built on the reef itself — which would have required dredging, foundation drilling, and the displacement of a documented 1,003 coral colonies in the construction footprint — was the first time the international surf media had to grapple with the question of who gets to decide what happens at this place. The local association *Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo*, led by Cindy Otcenasek, and the petition campaign led by local surfer Matahi Drollet, did not stop the tower. They did force a 25 percent reduction in its footprint, a public reckoning with the coral cost of a four-day television event, and a precedent that will outlast the Games themselves.

This page is structured around two ideas. First, the bathymetry — the specific, freakish geometry that produces this wave and no other. Second, the village — the community, the politics, the indigenous Tahitian context that the surf-media framing has historically erased. If you read only one section, read the second.
favela_note:
**The honest context.** Teahupoʻo is not a beach you visit. It is a village that became a brand. The road ends here; the surf media starts here. For the four days every August when the WSL or the Olympics are in town, the channel fills with motor yachts, the sky fills with helicopters, and a place with one grocery store hosts roughly its own population again in television crews and surfers' families.

The rest of the year, Teahupoʻo is a fishing village whose residents — the *ʻāʻai* fishermen, the small farms inland, the families who have been on this peninsula for generations — drive an hour and a half to Papeete for any errand more complex than groceries. The wave they share with the world breaks just past the reef, but the wave's economic value flows mostly to people who are not from here.

The 2024 Olympics judges' tower fight was, at one level, about coral. At another level it was about who gets to decide what counts as a "legacy." The tower's defenders — Paris 2024, the French Polynesian government, the WSL — said a permanent structure was needed to host future Tahiti Pros. *Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo* and the Drollet petition (200,000+ signatures, including Kelly Slater's endorsement) pointed out that the wooden tower the WSL had used since 1997 had hosted every previous Tahiti Pro just fine. The compromise that got built — 25 percent smaller footprint than the original aluminum proposal, but still drilled into the reef — is the unsatisfying middle answer most international sporting controversies end up at: a real concession, not a reversal.

If you come here to watch the wave, the small things matter. Taxi-boat operators are local. Tip in cash. Don't crowd the channel during the morning surf window. The post-Olympics MEGA Lab reef reassessment is ongoing as of early 2026; the comprehensive damage-vs-baseline report has not been published. The community story is still being written.

day_in_time.dawn: Pre-light at Teahupoʻo is the trade wind quiet. The PK 0 sign at the road's end is reflective in the headlights of one or two pickup trucks; the boat-launch ramp at the small marina has its first pre-dawn visitors — taxi-boat captains running pre-flight checks before the morning lineup heads out. The grocery store opens at 5:30. By the time the sun comes over the Tahiti Iti ridgeline behind the village, the channel is already populated: photographer boats taking position, two or three competitive surfers paddling into a still-glassy lineup. If there's swell, the sound carries — a deep thump every 12 to 15 seconds that you don't hear so much as feel in your sternum from a kilometer inland.
day_in_time.midday: Midday in the village is the slowest hour of the day. School-aged kids cross the one main road on bikes; the ʻāʻai market sells the morning's tuna and parrotfish; the small Catholic chapel on the lagoon side is quiet. At the surf, midday is when the trade wind picks up and the wave becomes harder to ride cleanly — most surf sessions are over by noon. The boats come back in to the marina; the photographers transfer their cards to laptops in the shade of the stilts under their accommodation. If there's no swell, midday in the channel is a turquoise mirror you can see thirty feet down through. The reef shelf is visible. From above, it looks gentle.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon on a no-swell day is the village's social hour: the lagoon-side seawall fills with families; teenagers fish from the rocks at the road's end; the small soccer pitch behind the marina hosts an informal game. On a swell day, the late afternoon belongs to the second-tier surfers — the locals and the visiting B-list who didn't get the morning slot — paddling the smaller, cleaner sets while the trade wind drops. The light at the channel just before the sun drops behind the Pacific horizon is the photograph everyone's tried to take. The wave silhouettes against the sky; the lip throws gold; the safety jet skis trace white wakes through the lagoon's blue.
day_in_time.night: After dark Teahupoʻo is rural Polynesia. There is no nightlife the way Papeete has nightlife. There is one small bar that's open on weekends; there are families on the front porches; there's the deep, constant sound of the reef break that you can hear from anywhere in the village if the swell is up. The Milky Way at this latitude, this far from any city light, is fully visible — the southern arms of it overhead, with the Magellanic Clouds low to the south. Most surfers are asleep by 9 p.m.; the village is asleep by 10. The PK 0 sign at the end of the road, lit by the truck-stop floodlight, is the closest thing this place has to a 24-hour landmark.

food_drink.name: Poisson cru à la tahitienne
food_drink.description: Tahitian raw fish — typically yellowfin tuna — cured briefly in lime juice and tossed with coconut milk, cucumber, tomato, and onion. The dish is served everywhere on the peninsula and is the closest thing French Polynesia has to a national dish. At Teahupoʻo specifically, the fish is often caught the same morning by ʻāʻai fishermen working the reef pass; the freshness is the difference between this version and the airport-restaurant version in Papeete.
food_drink.where: Snack Mahanai (village), Vahaiarii (Vairao), and most family-run pensions in Taiarapu-Ouest. Best at lunch.

food_drink.name: Mahi-mahi grillé
food_drink.description: Grilled mahi-mahi (dorade coryphène) is the second pillar of Tahitian coastal cooking. Usually served with steamed taro or breadfruit and a coconut-cream sauce. Mahi season runs roughly October to April, overlapping the southern-hemisphere off-season for surf, which is why most surf-tourism trip reports lean on poisson cru rather than mahi.
food_drink.where: Most pensions and the Pension Bonjouir overlooking the lagoon. Reservations sometimes required.

food_drink.name: Fei (red banana)
food_drink.description: Fei is a starchy red banana cultivated in the Society Islands for centuries, eaten roasted or boiled, never raw. It is heavier and starchier than commercial bananas and has a savory, almost potato-like character. You will see it served as a side dish in pensions; it is harder to find in Papeete restaurants but normal on the peninsula.
food_drink.where: Pension Bonjouir, family-run accommodations, occasionally the Vairao market.

food_drink.name: Hinano
food_drink.description: Tahiti's local lager, brewed in Papeete by Brasserie de Tahiti since 1955. The naming and label art (a Tahitian woman with a flower behind her ear, originally from a 1950s painting by Paul Jacoulet) is iconic across French Polynesia. Most surf trip footage of post-session beers at Teahupoʻo features Hinano green-bottle 50 cl bottles. Approximately 5 percent ABV.
food_drink.where: Every shop and bar in Taiarapu-Ouest. Cold from the snack at the road's end is the canonical post-surf version.

timeline.title: Joseph Banks documents Polynesian wave-riding in Tahiti
timeline.description: Aboard HMS *Endeavour* on James Cook's first voyage, naturalist Joseph Banks records the first Western description of Tahitian wave-riding — canoes ridden stern-first into the surf at Matavai Bay (the north shore of Tahiti Nui, ~70 km from Teahupoʻo). This entry is the earliest documented evidence of Polynesian heʻe nalu in any written language. It does not describe Passe Havae specifically, but establishes that wave-riding was an active Tahitian cultural practice at the time of European contact.
timeline.source: Banks's journal, 29 May 1769; reproduced at surfresearch.com.au

timeline.title: Thierry Vernaudon and friends surf Passe Havae for the first time
timeline.description: A small group of Tahitian surfers led by Thierry Vernaudon paddle out to the wave at Passe Havae and ride it in modest size. This is the first documented surfing of the modern Teahupoʻo break. Hawaiian bodyboarders Mike Stewart and Ben Severson follow the next year, helping seed the spot's underground reputation in the international surf community.
timeline.source: SurferToday, History of the Teahupoʻo wave

timeline.title: First WQS event held at Teahupoʻo
timeline.description: A four-star WQS qualifier is held at the wave. The swell is modest; the contest is more notable as a logistical proof-of-concept than as a competitive event. It establishes the wooden judges' tower the WSL would use for the next 27 years.
timeline.source: SurferToday, History of the Teahupoʻo wave

timeline.title: Gotcha Tahiti Pro — first contest in full-size conditions
timeline.description: The Gotcha Tahiti Pro hits the wave at full size. Footage and stills from the event circulate globally; this is the moment Teahupoʻo enters mainstream surf consciousness. The wave goes from underground reputation to international destination over a single contest window.
timeline.source: SurferToday, History of the Teahupoʻo wave

timeline.title: First Championship Tour event — Mark Occhilupo wins
timeline.description: The Billabong Pro Tahiti debuts on the WCT (now WSL CT). Australian Mark Occhilupo wins, becoming the first World Tour champion at Teahupoʻo. The wave is now a fixture on the year's surf calendar.
timeline.source: Atlantik Surf, Teahupoʻo winners in history

timeline.title: Brice Taerea dies after wipeout
timeline.description: 27 April 2000: Tahitian surfer Brice Taerea, caught inside on a 12-foot set, attempts to duck-dive a wave and is thrown over the falls onto the reef. He breaks two cervical vertebrae and severs his spinal cord. He is recovered alive but does not regain consciousness in hospital. He is the only confirmed surfing fatality at Teahupoʻo to date. The Gotcha Tahiti Pro is held one week later in his memory.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Teahupoʻo; SurferToday, Notable deaths in surfing

timeline.title: Laird Hamilton's "Millennium Wave" — 17 August
timeline.description: On a heavy August swell, Laird Hamilton tows in to a Teahupoʻo wave that photographer Tim McKenna captures at 11:38 a.m. local. Hamilton rides on his backside, deep in the cylinder. The image becomes the magazine and editorial centerpiece of the year and resets the international perception of what is rideable at this wave. McKenna's stills, taken from a water-level position in the channel, are still the standard for what the wave looks like at full size.
timeline.source: SurferToday, What is the Millennium Wave?; Tim McKenna's 25-year retrospective

timeline.title: Andy Irons wins the Billabong Pro — three months before his death
timeline.description: Andy Irons wins the 2010 Billabong Pro Tahiti, his second Teahupoʻo title. He dies three months later (2 November 2010) in a Dallas hotel room, age 32. The Tahiti win is one of the last great competitive performances of his career and is remembered as a Teahupoʻo result more than a season result.
timeline.source: Atlantik Surf, Teahupoʻo winners; SurferToday, Andy Irons 2010

timeline.title: Code Red — 27 August
timeline.description: An exceptional southern-hemisphere swell hits the wave at sizes large enough that the contest is put on hold and a Code Red advisory is issued, clearing the water of all but the most experienced. The day produces some of the largest and most heavily-broadcast Teahupoʻo footage on record, including segments that anchor Taylor Steele's documentary *This Time Tomorrow*.
timeline.source: Surfline, Code Red II coverage; The Inertia, Rastovich Code Red wave

timeline.title: First WSL Championship Tour women's event
timeline.description: The Outerknown Tahiti Pro becomes the first WCT women's event held at Teahupoʻo. The decision ends a 25-year run in which only men competed at the wave at the top professional level. The event format mirrors the men's contest, including same-day window logistics and judging tower placement.

timeline.title: Olympic tower barge breaks coral — construction paused
timeline.description: A barge mobilizing equipment for the new permanent aluminum Olympic judges' tower snags and breaks a section of coral at the construction site. Paris 2024 suspends work pending review. The MEGA Lab — a Hawaii-based marine science nonprofit working with the University of Hawaiʻi and the local association *Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo* — uses 3D photogrammetry to baseline the affected reef and identifies 1,003 coral colonies across 20 species in the proposed tower footprint. Their assessment warns dredging and construction could affect up to 2,500 square meters of reef and result in at least $1.3 million USD in direct ecosystem damage.
timeline.source: CNN, December 2023; University of Hawaiʻi News, March 2024

timeline.title: ISA proposes scrapping the tower — French Polynesia rejects
timeline.description: On 19 December 2023, the International Surfing Association (then led by Fernando Aguerre) publicly proposes cancelling the tower entirely and judging the event remotely from land, water, and drone cameras. The proposal is rejected by the French Polynesian government, which argues a permanent tower is needed for legacy use after the Games. The decision sets the stage for the eventual smaller-but-still-built compromise.

timeline.title: Vahine Fierro wins the SHISEIDO Tahiti Pro — first Tahitian women's champion
timeline.description: Competing as a wildcard for the third time, Tahitian-raised surfer Vahine Fierro wins the women's Tahiti Pro in 6-10 ft conditions. She is the first Tahitian (and the first wildcard) to win the women's event at her home break. She defeats Tatiana Weston-Webb in the semifinal even after Weston-Webb scores a perfect 10. Fierro was born in Huahine; her family relocated to Teahupoʻo so she and her sisters could pursue competitive surfing.
timeline.source: WSL official; SurferToday, Fierro wins 2024 Tahiti Pro

timeline.title: Kauli Vaast wins Olympic gold for France — 5 August
timeline.description: Kauli Vaast, born in Vairao on 26 February 2002, wins the Olympic gold medal at his home break with a final score of 17.67. He defeats Australia's Jack Robinson (silver, 7.83). Vaast started surfing at age 4 and is widely nicknamed "the King of Teahupoʻo." Caroline Marks of the United States wins the women's gold the same week. The gold medal is the first French men's surfing gold in Olympic history.
timeline.source: Olympics.com, Paris 2024 surfing men France Kauli Vaast gold

zones.name: Teahupoʻo village (PK 0)
zones.character: The fishing village at the literal end of the road. One main street, one grocery store (Snack Mahanai), one Catholic chapel, the public pier, the small marina, and the PK 0 marker. Year-round population ~1,455. Most pensions are family-owned and on the lagoon side of the road.
zones.best_for: Walking the road from PK 0 to the pier. Watching the fishermen unload at midday. The chapel is open. The marina is the staging point for taxi-boat trips out to the wave.
zones.notes: There are no traffic lights on the entire peninsula past Vairao. Drivers stop for pedestrians; tip the local taxi-boat captains; buy lunch from the village snacks rather than packing it in.

zones.name: Passe Havae — the wave
zones.character: The reef pass where the wave breaks. Approximately 800 m offshore from the village, west-southwest. The pass itself is a freshwater-cut channel through the barrier reef, eroded over millennia by streams from the ~4,000-ft Tahiti Iti ridge. To the surfer's right, the deep channel runs alongside the reef shelf and stays unbroken; to the surfer's left, the wave folds onto the inside reef where water depth at the lip impact zone can be as little as 51 cm.
zones.best_for: Watching from a taxi-boat in the channel. Photography from the channel-shoulder position is the canonical Teahupoʻo angle.
zones.notes: Do NOT swim or snorkel in the impact zone, even on a flat day; reef cuts here are deep and the medical facility is in Vairao. The channel itself is calm but is a working corridor for safety boats and surf photographers — small recreational craft are expected to stay clear of paddle-out lanes during contest windows.

zones.name: Public marina & boat launch
zones.character: The municipal small-boat marina at the village, base of operations for the local taxi-boat operators (Tahiti Surfari, Teahupoo Adventures, and several smaller family-run charters). Pre-dawn launch traffic is the heartbeat of every swell day. The marina also serves the ʻāʻai fishing fleet — small outboards working the reef and the deeper banks beyond.
zones.best_for: Booking a channel viewing trip. Most operators take cash; some take cards. Best to book a day in advance during contest windows.
zones.notes: Operators are local and family-run. Tip in cash. Do not negotiate aggressively — the rates posted are already accommodation-friendly for visitors and most of the income stays in the village.

zones.name: Fenua ʻAihere — the road's-end wilderness
zones.character: Beyond the PK 0 marker, the south coast of Tahiti Iti continues as the *Fenua ʻAihere* — "the wild land" — accessible only on foot or by boat. A roughly 20 km stretch of undeveloped coast with two small black-sand coves, freshwater streams running off the Tahiti Iti ridge, a lighthouse at Pari, and the only land approach to the cliff-foot caves at Te Pari. Multi-day hikes through here are a recognized adventure-tourism category and require local guides.
zones.best_for: Multi-day guided hike along the south coast (typically 3 days, with a return by boat to Tautira on the north coast). Day-trip kayak access from the marina to the first cove (~5 km).
zones.notes: Do not attempt without a local guide — there is no mobile signal, no marked trail past the first kilometer, and the rock at the cliff bases is consistently slick. Multi-day hikes book through Vairao-based outfitters.

landmarks.name: PK 0 — the end-of-the-road marker
landmarks.description: The kilometer-zero post at the end of the south-coast road of Tahiti Iti. The road from Papeete (~70 km) terminates here; beyond it the coast is the Fenua ʻAihere wilderness, accessible only on foot or by boat. The PK 0 sign is the most-photographed civic landmark in the village and the canonical "end of the world" framing for Teahupoʻo trip reports.

landmarks.name: Olympic judges' tower
landmarks.description: The aluminum judges' tower built for the 2024 Olympic surfing event, replacing the long-standing wooden WSL tower used since 1997. The original Paris 2024 design was a three-story, ~322 m² structure with full air-conditioning, toilets, and capacity for ~40 people. After community opposition led by *Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo* and a 200,000-signature petition campaign led by Matahi Drollet (with Kelly Slater's endorsement), the design was reduced 25 percent in footprint to roughly the original wooden tower's ~150 m², with capacity 25-30. The foundations are still drilled into the reef. The tower is collapsible and is intended for re-use at future Tahiti Pro events.

landmarks.name: Église Saint-Louis de Teahupoʻo
landmarks.description: Small Catholic chapel on the lagoon side of the village's main street. Modest concrete construction, tin roof, painted blue trim. Active congregation; Sunday mass is in French and Tahitian. Open during daylight hours.

landmarks.name: Tahiti Iti volcanic ridge (Mont Ronui)
landmarks.description: The 4,000-ft volcanic spine of Tahiti Iti rises directly behind the village. Mont Ronui (1,332 m) is the highest peak. The freshwater streams that drain the ridge are what carved Passe Havae through the barrier reef over millennia. The ridge is also the rain-shadow line: the south coast (where Teahupoʻo is) is drier than the north coast.

cultural_refs.title: Riding Giants
cultural_refs.creator: Stacy Peralta (dir.)
cultural_refs.description: Big-wave surfing documentary that includes a substantial Teahupoʻo segment built around Laird Hamilton's Millennium Wave. The film was the first mainstream cinema treatment of the wave and is the entry-point reference for most non-surfers' awareness of Teahupoʻo.

cultural_refs.title: This Time Tomorrow
cultural_refs.creator: Taylor Steele (dir.)
cultural_refs.description: Surf film following the 27 August 2011 Code Red swell across the Pacific. Includes the heavy Teahupoʻo session and Dave Rastovich's unbroadcast wave. Often cited as the canonical visual record of the largest paddle-and-tow day ever held at the wave.

cultural_refs.title: Children of Teahupoʻo
cultural_refs.creator: Tim McKenna
cultural_refs.description: 36-minute film featuring photographer Tim McKenna's images and short interviews with Tahitian surfers who grew up at Passe Havae. The film foregrounds local voices that surf media usually skips.

cultural_refs.title: Teahupoʻo Surf Camp: Road to Paris 2024
cultural_refs.creator: Olympic Channel
cultural_refs.description: Olympic Channel original documentary following Olympic hopefuls in the run-up to the 2024 Games at Teahupoʻo. Includes interviews with Kauli Vaast, Vahine Fierro, and Tahitian surf families.

cultural_refs.title: Laird Hamilton, Millennium Wave (17 August 2000)
cultural_refs.creator: Tim McKenna
cultural_refs.description: The single most-reproduced still image in modern surf photography. Shot from the channel at 11:38 a.m. local on 17 August 2000, capturing Laird Hamilton tow-in surfing on his backside deep in the cylinder. Reset international perception of what a rideable wave could look like.

cultural_refs.title: Save Teahupoʻo / Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo Olympic tower campaign
cultural_refs.creator: Matahi Drollet, Cindy Otcenasek, Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo
cultural_refs.description: Public campaign opposing the Paris 2024 Olympic plan to drill foundations for a permanent aluminum judges' tower into the Teahupoʻo reef. Combined petitions reached 200,000+ signatures, including Kelly Slater's endorsement. The campaign did not stop the tower, but forced a 25-percent footprint reduction and a public scientific reckoning (via the MEGA Lab baseline study) with the coral cost of the event.

recurring_events.name: Tahiti Pro / SHISEIDO Tahiti Pro
recurring_events.description: WSL Championship Tour event at Teahupoʻo. Held in a 10-day swell window each southern-hemisphere autumn. Both men's and women's CT events have run here since 2022; men's since 1999. Free to watch from shore; channel viewing requires a taxi-boat charter.
recurring_events.typical_attendance: WSL credentialed media + ~1,000 visiting spectators across the event window

recurring_events.name: Big-wave swell window
recurring_events.description: Teahupoʻo's primary swell season is the Southern Hemisphere winter, when storms in the Roaring Forties south of New Zealand and across the Southern Ocean send long-period groundswells north into French Polynesia. The wave can break at heights from 6 ft (small/contestable) to 25+ ft (Code Red advisories). Smaller swells run year-round but are far less consistent outside this window.

recurring_events.name: Heiva i Tahiti — village delegation
recurring_events.description: The Heiva i Tahiti is the major annual Polynesian cultural festival held in Papeete each July, with traditional dance, song, sport (vaʻa canoe racing, javelin, stone-lifting), and regional pageantry. Taiarapu-Ouest commune (which includes Teahupoʻo) sends a delegation. The Heiva is not held in Teahupoʻo itself, but it is the cultural rhythm of the year for many local families.

businesses.name: Snack Mahanai
businesses.description: The village snack and grocery — café, takeaway, and small store rolled into one. Open daily from 5:30 a.m.; serves poisson cru, casse-croûte sandwiches, Hinano beer, and basic groceries. The pre-dawn opening is the staging point for surfers heading out on the first taxi-boat run.

businesses.name: Tahiti Surfari
businesses.description: Local taxi-boat operator running surf-watching trips from the village marina to the channel adjacent to Passe Havae. Typical trip: 2-4 hours, morning slot. Family-owned, boat captains are local Tahitian residents.

businesses.name: Teahupoo Adventures
businesses.description: Second of the established taxi-boat operators serving the channel viewing market. Also offers Fenua ʻAihere coastal kayak trips and multi-day guided hikes.

businesses.name: Pension Bonjouir
businesses.description: Small lagoon-side pension on the road into the village, run by a local family. Twelve rooms, breakfast included, restaurant on site serving traditional Tahitian dishes (mahi-mahi, poisson cru, fei). The pension is a 5-minute drive from the marina.
businesses.address: Route Teahupoʻo, between Vairao and the village

businesses.name: Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo
businesses.description: Local community association based in Teahupoʻo, focused on coastal stewardship, cultural preservation, and public oversight of large-scale infrastructure projects affecting the reef. President: Cindy Otcenasek. Led the principal community opposition to the original Paris 2024 aluminum judges' tower design and continues to monitor reef impact in the post-Games period.

businesses.name: MEGA Lab (research partner)
businesses.description: Hawaii-based marine science nonprofit that conducted the pre-Olympics baseline reef survey of the Passe Havae construction footprint. Used 3D photogrammetry to identify and catalog 1,003 coral colonies across 20 species in the proposed tower site. Partnered with the University of Hawaiʻi and Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo. Their post-Games reef damage reassessment is in progress as of early 2026; the comprehensive report has not yet been published.

spike_explainer:
The reason Teahupoʻo is spoken about the way it is has nothing to do with size and everything to do with geometry. Off most of the world's reefs and points, an incoming swell feels the bottom gradually — it 'shoals,' slowing and standing up over a shelf that bleeds off some of its energy before it breaks. Passe Havae has no shelf. The seafloor here drops from roughly a thousand feet of open Pacific to about six feet of coral over the span of a few hundred yards, and the swell arrives still carrying nearly all of its deep-water energy. It does not have time to shoal. It folds.

What that fold produces is a wave that looks physically wrong. The crest stays up near sea level while the trough is scooped out below it, so the surfer drops into a pit whose floor sits beneath the flat water behind the wave. The lip that pitches over is as thick as the wave is tall — a moving slab of water metres deep — and it lands on a reef that at the impact zone can be as little as 51 centimetres beneath the surface. This is why Teahupoʻo is called not the biggest wave in the world but the heaviest. Nazaré is far taller; Cortes Bank moves more water in the abstract. Teahupoʻo concentrates its power into the smallest, shallowest, most consequential space of any wave people are willing to ride.

The pass itself is the work of fresh water. Streams draining the roughly 4,000-foot ridge of Tahiti Iti have cut a channel through the barrier reef over millennia, and that channel is what makes the wave rideable at all: to the surfer's right runs deep, unbroken water — the escape route — while to the left the wave detonates onto the shallow inside reef. Get it right and you exit into the channel. Get it wrong and the coral is directly beneath you. On 27 April 2000 the Tahitian surfer Briece Taerea, caught inside on a twelve-foot set, tried to duck-dive under a wave, was thrown over the falls, and landed head-first on the reef; he broke two cervical vertebrae and severed his spinal cord, and remains the wave's only confirmed surfing fatality. Every serious surfer here treats the reef, not the wave, as the thing that will hurt them.

For a wave this famous, its modern history is short. The first documented surfing of Passe Havae was in 1985, when the Tahitian Thierry Vernaudon paddled out with a few friends; Hawaiian bodyboarders followed and seeded its underground reputation. The first contest came in 1997, on the wooden judges' tower the WSL would use for the next twenty-seven years. Then, on 17 August 2000, Laird Hamilton was towed into a wave that the photographer Tim McKenna shot from the channel — the 'Millennium Wave,' the single most reproduced image in modern surf photography, and the moment the wave became globally legible. The Code Red swell of 27 August 2011 pushed the size envelope further still. Kelly Slater has won the Tahiti Pro five times; the women's Championship Tour finally arrived in 2022.

In 2024 the wave became something else again: an Olympic venue, some 15,800 kilometres from Paris, the most distant host-to-venue pairing in the Games' history. It could hardly have gone better for the place itself. Vahine Fierro, a Tahitian competing as a wildcard, won the women's Tahiti Pro in May — the first Tahitian ever to do so. Then on 5 August, Kauli Vaast, born just up the coast in Vairao and nicknamed the King of Teahupoʻo, won the Olympic gold medal at his home break. A wave named, in one reading of its Tahitian roots, for a wall of skulls had produced a local champion on the largest stage there is.

But the Games are also why the wave is now framed as a question rather than a spectacle. To judge the event, Paris 2024 proposed replacing the old wooden tower with a permanent three-story aluminium structure drilled into the reef — a plan a baseline survey by the Hawaii-based MEGA Lab found would sit atop 1,003 documented coral colonies across twenty species. In December 2023 a construction barge broke a section of that reef and work was paused. The local association Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo, led by Cindy Otcenasek, and a petition campaign led by the local surfer Matahi Drollet that gathered more than 200,000 signatures, did not stop the tower. They forced it 25 percent smaller — and forced the international surf world to answer, for the first time, a question the sport had always dodged: who gets to decide what happens to this reef, and whether a four-day television event is worth drilling into it.

That is the real spike at Teahupoʻo. The wave is a piece of freak bathymetry that produces the heaviest ride on Earth, and it rewards being understood on those terms. But it breaks off the end of a one-lane road, past a village of 1,455 people who share it with the world and keep little of what it earns. Read the geometry, and then read the village — because the most important thing about Teahupoʻo is no longer only what the wave does to a surfer, but what the world does to the wave.
reckoning_pullquote: Teahupoʻo is not a beach you visit. It is a village that became a brand — and the wave's value flows mostly to people who are not from here.

margin_notes.text: No continental shelf softens the swell: the bottom climbs from about a thousand feet to six feet in a few hundred yards, so the wave folds instead of shoaling, its lip landing over as little as 51 cm of coral.
Modern surfing here began only in 1985 (Thierry Vernaudon). Laird Hamilton's 17 August 2000 'Millennium Wave' made it globally legible; the Tahitian Briece Taerea died on the reef that same year.

The 2024 Olympic judges' tower would have sat on 1,003 catalogued coral colonies. Vai Ara o Teahupoʻo and Matahi Drollet's 200,000-signature petition forced it 25 percent smaller — but it was still drilled into the reef.

things_to_know.label: You need a boat
things_to_know.note: The wave breaks about 800 m offshore over Passe Havae and can't be seen from the village. Watching means chartering a local taxi-boat from the marina — cash, and book a day ahead during contest windows.

things_to_know.label: The reef is lethal
things_to_know.note: The lip breaks over as little as 51 cm of sharp coral. Never swim or snorkel the impact zone even on a flat day; the wave's only confirmed surfing death, Briece Taerea (2000), happened on this reef.

things_to_know.label: End of the road
things_to_know.note: The sealed road ends at the PK 0 marker; beyond it the Fenua ʻAihere coast is foot- or boat-access only. Papeete and the airport are about 70 km (1.5 hours) away.

things_to_know.label: Swell season
things_to_know.note: Southern-Hemisphere winter (roughly April to October) brings the groundswells; the wave ranges from a contestable 6 ft to 25+ ft 'Code Red' days.

things_to_know.label: It is a living village
things_to_know.note: About 1,455 residents, one grocery, one chapel. Tip boat captains in cash, don't crowd the channel during the morning surf window, and buy your lunch in the village.

things_to_know.label: Getting there
things_to_know.note: Fly into Faaʻa International (PPT) near Papeete, then drive about 70 km to the end of the Tahiti Iti south-coast road.

access_note: Teahupoʻo sits at the end of the south-coast road of Tahiti Iti, about 70 km (1.5 hours) from Papeete and Faaʻa International Airport (PPT). The sealed road terminates at the PK 0 marker; the wave breaks roughly 800 m offshore over Passe Havae and can only be seen from a boat. Local taxi-boat operators at the village marina run channel-viewing trips (cash, book a day ahead during contest windows). Beyond PK 0, the Fenua ʻAihere coast is reachable only on foot or by boat, with local guides.
